EchoMQ / The Cache

← the depth behind redis-patterns R1 · Caching

The Cache · pillar III

Serve reads
from the near-cache

Answer from memory when you can, fall through to the store when you must — and never let a herd fill it twice.

The Cache is the third pillar, and it stands in front of the other two. Where the Queue distributes work and the Bus broadcasts signals, the Cache serves reads. It is a declared near-cache — an L1 ETS table in front of the L2 Valkey the bus already runs on — read cache-aside: a hit is a caller-side :ets.lookup that never enters the owning process, and the owner is consulted only on a miss, where the second law holds: one fill per herd. Concurrent misses coalesce onto a single loader; expiry is jittered so no cohort dies together; and a full cache degrades to pass-through, it never fails. Every value is framed with its writer's mint-time version — the seed of the coherence that keeps the caches honest. It is all real code, grounded in echo/apps/echo_store.

The read, cache-aside The modules The rest of the system References

The read, cache-aside

A cache-aside read tries the layers in order and stops at the first that answers. The fast path never leaves the caller: a hit is a lookup in a public ETS table, in your process, costing nothing but the lookup. Only a miss consults the owner — and there a miss in front of a thundering herd becomes one fill, not a thousand. Pick a path to read what happens, the Valkey command beneath it, and the source the fetch/3 answer is tagged with.

One read, tried in order: L1 answers in your own process, L2 answers from the shared store, the loader answers once for the whole herd — and the clock, jittered, makes sure no cohort ever asks again all at once.

The modules

Three modules and the closing workshop are ready — each module a hub and three dives, the workshop one end-to-end build. Each grounds in the real echo/apps/echo_store code — the declared tiers and the cache-aside read, then single-flight and the jittered clock that keep it cheap under a herd and bounded under pressure.

built page ready to read soon on the build front
01built

Cache-aside, two layers

The declared near-cache: L1 ETS over L2 Valkey on ecc:{q}:id, the cache-aside fetch/3, and the version-framed write.
02built

Single-flight & jittered TTL

One loader for a thundering herd; cohorts that never expire in step; a full cache that degrades to pass-through, never to failure.
03built

Coherence — newer wins on the Bus

A message about a name: two identities, the eleven-byte mint comparison, the broadcast and job lanes, server-assisted tracking.
04built

Workshop

Hit at ETS speed, survive a herd with one fill, watch a jittered cohort expire, then invalidate from another node.

Redis Patterns Applied

This is the depth behind the Redis Patterns Applied chapters that door here: R1 · Caching — the cache-aside, stampede-prevention, and session patterns this pillar applies in real code, and R5 · Streams & Events — the bus-coherent invalidation that keeps a versioned fill current. There the pattern is the door; here is the near-cache it names.

The version on every row

A cache that serves a read must also learn when that read goes stale. Every value here is framed with its writer's mint-time version — a 14-byte branded id stored as version <> value — and coherence is the rule that compares two such versions and lets the newer win, with no coordinator and no clock but the one inside every id. That is the third module. The manuscript chapter these figures realize is The Branded Component System · the Store; the durable floor a coherence job survives a crash on is Echo Persistence.

The rest of the system

The Cache is one reading of the shared substrate. The Queue distributes work over the same wire; the Bus broadcasts signals and keeps a replayable log; the Proof chapter shows the whole system holds. The Protocol below them all — the keyspace the ecc: prefix stands beside — is ready to read now.

←built

The Protocol

The substrate the Cache stands beside: the braced keyspace, the record, the Lua layer, the branded-id gate.
Ibuilt

The Queue

Distribute work: the lifecycle, jobs, lanes, the consumer, batches, flows.
IIbuilt

The Bus

Broadcast signals: fire-and-forget events and a retained, replayable log ordered by mint.
IVsoon

The Proof

The system holds: the conformance suite, telemetry, the benchmark gate.

The Protocol, the Queue, the Bus, and the Cache are ready to read; the Proof is on the build front, added as its pages land.

References

Sources

  • Erlang/OTP — the ets module — the public, read-concurrent L1 table a hit reads directly, in the caller's process.
  • Valkey — Cluster specification — the {table} hashtag lands every ecc: key of one cache on one of 16384 slots.
  • Söderqvist — A new hash table — the L2 Valkey the near-cache fronts, costed at rest.
  • Helland — Life Beyond Distributed Transactions — the entity addressed by a key, cached close to where it is used.
  • King — Announcing Snowflake — the time-ordered id every cached value is framed with.

Related in this course

  • The Bus — the wire a coherence message broadcasts over.
  • The Queue — the fair lanes a crash-surviving coherence job rides.
  • The Protocol — the keyspace and branded-id gate the ecc: prefix stands beside.
  • The Branded Component System · the Store — the manuscript chapter this pillar realizes.
  • Echo Persistence — the durable floor beneath the volatile tiers.
